text.title: Multi-Channel Content Repurposing
text.summary: One differentiated long-form piece generates five channel-native variants: blog post, LinkedIn article, Twitter thread, email newsletter, and video script — each restructured for its channel's conventions, not just reformatted.

rule.statement: Repurpose content by restructuring for channel conventions, not by cutting sentences from the original. A 1500-word blog post becomes a Twitter thread by selecting the most specific data points and restructuring — not by trimming.
rule.rationale: AI repurposing agents that summarize for social media pick the least differentiated points (generic takeaways) rather than the most differentiated (specific data). Explicit restructuring instructions produce channel-native content; format-swap produces bland extracts.

list.item: Blog post (2500 words): full methodology, case studies, data deep-dive, video embeds, internal linking.
LinkedIn article (400 words): hook with contrarian take, 3-5 key points, professional tone, link to full blog.
Twitter/X thread (7-8 tweets): one key finding per tweet, statistics, curiosity hook in first tweet, CTA in last.
Email newsletter (250 words): curiosity-hook subject line, preview of proprietary data, 1-2 key insights, CTA to full post.
Video script (3 min): same structure as thread, visual descriptions for each point, CTA at end.

rule.statement: Build a content asset library of approved expert quotes, proprietary data points, and case studies that agents pull from when generating repurposed variants.
rule.rationale: Without a curated asset library, AI repurposing agents fall back to generic statements. A library of specific, pre-approved differentiation elements mechanically raises the quality floor of every repurposed piece.
